resume_snapshot.last_user_message:
You are working in my Claude Code workspace. I want you to auto‑tune the Stop hook system using past transcripts, now that the hook aggregation layer is in place.

Do NOT ask me to manually rate examples. Use existing transcripts and simple heuristics.

Goal
Reduce noise while preserving effectiveness by:

lowering severity / confidence for hooks that often fire on “good enough” answers,

identifying patterns where multiple hooks fire on the same root issue and should be suppressed,

tightening or relaxing certain gates based on how they behave on real historical data.

The focus is global analysis quality, not just fabrication/safety.

Inputs you should use
The existing hook & aggregator setup:

Stop.py

Stop_router.py

Stop_aggregator.py (already classifying ~130 hooks into 12 root issues, deduplicating, prioritizing, rendering).

Historical transcripts:

Any conversation logs available locally that include:

user messages,

model messages,

Stop hook outputs (raw hook results and aggregated issues, if logged).

If there is an existing transcript/log directory, find it. If not, search the workspace for files named like *_transcript.json, logs/, history/, or similar.

Part 1 — Discover transcript sources
Locate where transcripts or logs live in this workspace.

Search for:

transcripts, logs, history, runs, conversations.

Confirm at least:

path,

approximate number of files,

format (JSON, JSONL, text).

If no machine-readable transcripts exist, stop and report that limitation.

Part 2 — Build an automatic evaluation pass
Assuming you find transcripts with both model outputs and hook results:

For each diagnostic/explanatory turn (use existing intent/intent_classifier if available):

Extract:

the model’s response text,

the aggregated issues from the new Stop_aggregator (if present), or reconstruct aggregation for that turn from raw hook outputs.

Use simple heuristics to classify each turn as likely OK or likely problematic without my help, for example:

Likely OK (good enough):

Answer contains:

at least one explicit citation or file reference,

at least one explicit uncertainty marker (“could be”, “likely”, “I’m not sure”),

and no user correction immediately following that contradicts the answer.

Likely problematic:

The very next user message is a strong correction ("you're wrong", "this is incorrect", "you didn't read X", etc.),

or the answer has zero citations, no uncertainty, AND includes strong causal claims.

This labeling is heuristic and imperfect, but that’s acceptable for tuning.

For each turn, build a small record:

json
{
  "turn_id": "...",
  "is_diagnostic": true/false,
  "heuristic_label": "ok" | "problematic",
  "aggregated_issues": [
    {"root_issue": "...", "severity": "block|warn|info", "confidence": "high|medium|low"}
  ]
}
Part 3 — Learn which root issues are noisy vs useful
Over all diagnostic/explanatory turns, compute:

for each root_issue:

how often it appears on heuristic_ok vs heuristic_problematic turns,

how often it co-occurs with other root issues (e.g., diagnostic_analysis_incomplete + lazy_closure),

whether its presence correlates with user corrections (proxy for real problems).

Use this to roughly classify each root_issue into:

strong signal (mostly on problematic turns),

mixed,

weak/noisy (frequent on ok turns, rare on problematic turns).

Do the same for combinations, e.g.:

diagnostic_analysis_incomplete + lazy_closure

unverified_stance + overconfidence

recommendation_followthrough when the next user message is not an acceptance/“do that”.

Part 4 — Auto-tune mapping in Stop_aggregator
Based on the statistics above, update Stop_aggregator.py to:

Adjust confidence and severity:

For root issues that mostly fire on heuristic_ok turns:

lower confidence,

demote severity from warn to info or telemetry-only where appropriate.

For root issues strongly correlated with heuristic_problematic turns:

keep or raise confidence,

preserve warn/block as appropriate.

Tighten suppression:

For common co-occurrence patterns:

if root_issue A and B almost always co-occur, and A is higher confidence, consider suppressing B when A is present.

Example:

If diagnostic_analysis_incomplete nearly always co-occurs with lazy_closure, and diagnostic_analysis_incomplete is more predictive of user corrections, prefer surfacing only that one.

Special-case obvious noise:

If recommendation_followthrough frequently fires on turns where:

the user did NOT ask to execute,

and the next user message is not an acceptance,

then lower its severity or restrict to cases where the model explicitly promised “I will do X” and did not.

If generic epistemic-format violations frequently appear on short meta turns (“Waiting for your next instruction”), down-weight or suppress them for meta/control turns.

Keep changes small and explicit: a handful of adjustments to the mapping table and suppression logic, not a full rewrite.

Part 5 — Re-run on a sample
After tuning, re-run the evaluation script on a smaller random sample of diagnostic turns and report:

For each root_issue:

before vs after:

frequency on heuristic_ok vs heuristic_problematic turns,

any obvious improvement in “signal vs noise”.

Example metrics (even approximate counts):

diagnostic_analysis_incomplete: 80% of warnings now appear on problematic turns vs 55% before.

recommendation_followthrough: 50% drop in warnings on ok/meta turns.

Part 6 — Report
Reply with:

FILES_CHANGED
list of files changed (likely the evaluation script + Stop_aggregator.py)

LOGIC
how you:

discovered transcripts,

labeled turns heuristically,

aggregated statistics,

updated root_issue mapping and suppression.

METRICS
per root_issue, rough before/after stats on:

% of firings on heuristic_ok vs heuristic_problematic turns,

any notable co-occurrence/suppression patterns you added.

LIMITATIONS
where the heuristics might mislabel turns,

any root issues you left unchanged due to insufficient evidence.

Do not claim changes or metrics unless you actually implemented and ran the evaluation in this workspace.
